docs.title: Module M18 Quiz: Economic Growth: The Solow Model
docs.text:
25 questions · Intermediate · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working before checking.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Growth

What is Kaldor's first stylised fact about economic growth?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Output per worker grows at a constant rate
  • B) Capital per worker declines over time
  • C) The capital-output ratio fluctuates wildly
  • D) Labour income share rises with development
Show Answer

Answer: A) Output per worker grows at a constant rate

Kaldor's first fact states output per worker grows at roughly constant rate over time.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Growth

Calculate labour share if GDP=\(2tr and labour compensation=\)1.3tr

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 0.65

Labour share = labour compensation/GDP = 1.3/2 = 0.65

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Intermediate

Briefly explain why Kaldor's facts matter for growth models

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Growth models must be consistent with these empirical regularities to be valid.

growth

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Growth

Which fact does Australia's mining boom violate?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Constant capital-output ratio
  • B) Constant labour income share
  • C) Constant rate of return
  • D) Varying growth rates across countries
Show Answer

Answer: A) Constant capital-output ratio

Mining boom caused K/Y to rise temporarily, violating Fact 3.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Growth

If GDP=1.7tr and capital income=0.65tr, what is α?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 0.38

α = capital income/GDP = 0.65/1.7 ≈ 0.38

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Growth

In Solow model, what does (δ+n+g)k represent?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Gross investment
  • B) Break-even investment
  • C) Consumption
  • D) Technological progress
Show Answer

Answer: B) Break-even investment

Break-even investment replaces depreciation and equips new effective workers.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Growth

Calculate y if k=5.771 and α=0.35

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 1.85

y = k^α = 5.771^0.35 ≈ 1.85

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Intermediate

Why does higher s not affect long-run growth rate?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Because long-run growth is driven by exogenous technological progress (g).

growth

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Growth

If s=0.3, δ=0.06, n=0.02, g=0.02, α=0.4, find k*

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 6.24

k* = [0.3/(0.06+0.02+0.02)]^(1/0.6) ≈ 6.24

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Growth

What happens if k > k*?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Economy grows faster
  • B) Capital decumulates
  • C) Consumption maximized
  • D) Technology regresses
Show Answer

Answer: B) Capital decumulates

When k > k*, break-even investment exceeds actual investment → k falls.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Growth

What does conditional convergence mean?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All countries converge to same income
  • B) Countries converge to their own steady states
  • C) Growth rates equalize
  • D) Technology diffuses instantly
Show Answer

Answer: B) Countries converge to their own steady states

Countries converge to their own k* based on parameters (s,n,etc).

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Growth

Calculate k̇ if k=4, s=0.25, α=0.35, δ+n+g=0.08

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 0.086

k̇ = s·k^α - (δ+n+g)k = 0.25·4^0.35 - 0.08·4 ≈ 0.086

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intermediate

Why is absolute convergence unrealistic?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Countries have different saving rates, population growth, etc.

growth

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Growth

What's Australia's approximate convergence speed?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) 2%/year
  • B) 5%/year
  • C) 10%/year
  • D) 15%/year
Show Answer

Answer: B) 5%/year

Speed ≈ (1-α)(δ+n+g) = 0.65×0.08 ≈ 5%/year

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Growth

If k₀=2, k*=5.771, growth rate of k?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 7.9%

k̇/k = (s·k^(α-1) - (δ+n+g)) = (0.25·2^(-0.65) - 0.08) ≈ 7.9%

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Growth

What saving rate maximizes c* in Cobb-Douglas?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) α
  • B) 1-α
  • C) δ+n+g
  • D) g
Show Answer

Answer: A) α

Golden rule saving rate s_GR = capital share α.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Growth

Calculate c if y=1.847 and (δ+n+g)k*=0.462

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 1.385

c = y - (δ+n+g)k* = 1.847 - 0.462 = 1.385

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intermediate

Why is s > α dynamically inefficient?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Because reducing s would increase both current and future consumption.

growth

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Growth

If α=0.4, δ=0.06, n=0.02, g=0.02, find k*_GR

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 10.08

k*_GR = [0.4/(0.06+0.02+0.02)]^(1/0.6) ≈ 10.08

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Growth

Australia's s=0.24 vs s_GR=0.35 implies:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Under-saving
  • B) Over-saving
  • C) Golden rule
  • D) Dynamic efficiency
Show Answer

Answer: A) Under-saving

s < s_GR means under-saving - could increase c* by saving more.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Growth

On BGP, Y/L grows at rate:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) g
  • B) n
  • C) s
  • D) δ
Show Answer

Answer: A) g

Output per worker grows at rate of technological progress (g).

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Growth

If g=0.015, calculate Y/L growth over 40 years

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 82.2%

Y/L ratio = e^(0.015×40) - 1 ≈ 82.2% growth

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Intermediate

Why does break-even investment include gk?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: To offset capital dilution from technological progress.

growth

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Growth

If n rises from 0.015 to 0.025 (Australia), effect on k*?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: -16.6%

Higher n increases break-even investment, reducing k* by ~16.6%.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Growth

Higher population growth reduces:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Y/L level but not growth
  • B) Y/L growth but not level
  • C) Both level and growth
  • D) Neither level nor growth
Show Answer

Answer: A) Y/L level but not growth

Reduces level of Y/L but long-run growth still determined by g.
